original_abstract: In nature, non-heme iron-containing enzymes leverage dioxygen to generate high-spin iron(IV)-oxo intermediates capable of executing challenging oxygenation reactions. While creating synthetic mimics of these biological catalysts has been a long-standing goal, the activation of $\text{O}_2$ to produce high-spin (S = 2) iron(IV)=O species has remained elusive in synthetic chemistry. Here, we report the synthesis and characterization of a metal–organic framework featuring iron(II) sites that architecturally mimic the active sites of $\alpha$-ketoglutarate-dependent dioxygenases. Upon exposure to $\text{O}_2$ at low temperatures, the framework facilitates the decarboxylation of an $\alpha$-keto acid co-ligand to form a high-spin iron(IV)=O species. This reactive intermediate was identified and characterized through a suite of spectroscopic techniques, including in situ diffuse reflectance infrared Fourier transform (DRIFTS), in situ and variable-field Mössbauer, Fe K$\beta$ X-ray emission, and nuclear resonance vibrational spectroscopies (NRVS). We demonstrate that this framework acts as a competent platform for the catalytic oxygenation of cyclohexane and the stoichiometric conversion of ethane to ethanol using dioxygen as the oxidant. This work provides a bridge between enzymatic catalysis and heterogeneous materials, demonstrating that the robust high-spin reactivity of nature’s oxygenases can be realized in a synthetic porous solid.
humanized_abstract: Nature’s non-heme iron-containing enzymes catalyze important dioxygen-activating oxygenation reactions in biology through the formation of high-spin iron(IV)-oxo intermediates. Development of synthetic analogues of these iron enzymes has long been an active area of research, however activation of $\text{O}_2$ to form a high-spin iron species in the (S = 2) state has not been achievable in synthetic complexes and catalysis. We present here the design, synthesis and $\text{O}_2$ activation of an iron metal-organic framework material that enables low-temperature activation of $\text{O}_2$ with the iron centre to be reduced and then decarboxylate $\alpha$-keto acid co-ligands to form a high-spin iron(IV)=O species. These intermediates were characterized through in situ DRIFTS, in situ and variable-field room temperature (RT) Mössbauer, Fe K$\beta$ X-ray emission and nuclear resonance vibrational spectroscopies (NRVS). Catalyst activation and catalytic performance using the iron material is demonstrated through turnover of cyclohexane with $\text{O}_2$ as well as through stoichiometric oxidation of ethane into ethanol using $\text{O}_2$ as the oxidant. Here we therefore present a study that not only bridges enzymatic catalysis and heterogeneous materials, but also is able to produce the same robust high-spin reactivity seen in oxygenases in a stable porous solid.

Catalyst activation and catalytic performance using the iron material is demonstrated through turnover of cyclohexane with $\text{O}_2$ as well as through stoichiometric oxidation of ethane into ethanol using $\text{O}_2$ as the oxidant.
